author: @maxheadrom3088
text:
Hello, sir. This video impressed me since the first time I saw it. I'll look for the gel recipes in your other videos.

I have one question: here in Brazil there's this "magic drip" that "makes soldering easier" and is "not corrosive". I got one and then found a review saying that it did not create good electric bonding - so I did not use it. It's a blue liquid and only a drop is required. It does give good mechanical bonding. Do you know what that is? Thanks!

author: @victoryfirst2878
text: Come on Robert. you know this would really work great with a heat exchanger for sure. Stainless to keep from oxidizing and copper for good heat exchange. Eazy Peazy done real easy. Works for me. V

author: @FolieduNord
text:
Hi, thank you for your interesting video's! For my RC car model projects I'm especially interested in your method of soldering copper onto stainless steel. 
But I really can't find your video ('s  ?) on how to make the coppering gel... ?! (And bytheway, I've noticed that in different video's where it's used it also seems to vary in colour from bright blue to light green..?)
I've noticed several others of your fans are struggling with this same issue... Could you please provide us fools with a simple link?

author: @RawEEGs
text:
Aluminum surface facing the sun, underneath lies a resistive material like glass, or diamond. On the other side stainless steel to absorb and transfer heat energy. Between the two a diode. Electrons carry heat, and that energy can be sucked out of light itself.

This layered material would reduce the need to mirror as aluminum is reflective, and could operate underneath the photovoltaic monocrystalline structure. The excess heat could then be transported away via Bernoulli's fluid dynamics principle. Forced Air Heater (imagine cutting the bottom off a beer bottle and stacking them continuously. As the air heats up it's pushed through the bottleneck [since the panels are already angled that way], and the air never goes back down the bottleneck). You could spin another small electric motor off of that for added Wattage.

author: @jpolowin0
text:
Hi, Robert.  Thanks for posting these! -- I'm working on a project that will require soldering copper wires to smallish bits of stainless, and this looks like it might do the trick.  I'll probably have to use something like a buffing tip on a Dremel to clean the surfaces.

What sort of concentration of FeCl3 are you using in that gel?  I've watched a number of your videos but haven't seen that mentioned.  I'll be having to approximate things anyway with homemade FeCl3, not to mention homemade CuCl2 if my small stock of CuSO4 runs out.  Yay, chemistry!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: chemistry rocks mate - unfortunately i can't give out exact formulas - this shows it can be done and experimentation is needed to round it off - i wish you the best of luck though and happy experiementing

replies.author: @jpolowin0
replies.text: Even an approximate formula would help, please.  Just FeCl3 in gelatin + water?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sure - why not - give it a go?

author: @petersloper9674
text: Rob - soldering to stainless is much easier than that... Get yourself a bottle of rust remover (couple of quid), degrease spoon with acetone, dab on the rust remover with a Q-tip and then apply solder with your regular iron.  Flows on like a dream :)

replies.author: @TheSubziMr
replies.text: Thanks for sharing! I'll give it a go when I get to that point!

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
soldering onto stainless would be good for connecting to antennas that have to be in contact with salty environments I suppose, what I am wondering is how can you use these processes for say graphene work, like getting copper to bond to the surface of a graphite block or strip and then solder onto that :)

kinda wondering if the copper plating process could be applied to metGlas *(a silicon based metal compound that has a very low retention for magnetism but high conductivity as a paramagnetic substance, so it makes amazing cores for say transformers. *high magnetic transference and very little energy when the power is reversed to overcome any residue magnetic force in the stuff* but it is neigh impossible to get any sort of soldering bond to the stuff with traditional methods,
I will drop a link here, not sure what it would take to get some as they are fairly restrictive over who can get the stuff and make you sign a waiver that says  you will not use it in any application related to weapons technology lol

the alloy I have tried to solder to was  2705M
http://www.metglas.com/products/magnetic_materials/

(should be about halfway down the page) and if you ask you might get them to send some samples, I got two for the asking, though I am in the USA, and with how odd they were about their restrictiveness and agreements I am not sure how they feel about sending it out of the US lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Thomas Anderson that definately looks worth a try mate - you can get metglas from an RCBO - it's in a coil in the white toroid looking thing - i will do a vid on it so you know what i mean

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: ah excellent, and glad you have access easily to it :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Thomas Anderson you might well too - i'll do the vid when i get home

author: @BluesDoctor
text: Robert Murray-Smith we had a production plastic housing w/electroless nickel coating technician soldered a lead to. An EMI issue. The girls did this all day long. I tried and tried but burned a hole every time. Never know when something like this has high value, but never underestimate the skills of craftsmanship. The guy who developed the process couldn't do it either. These little tid-bits are great to have in the tool box to solve tough problems. Thanks so much for all your tech contributions making the world a better place for many aspiring technologists.

replies.author: @gordslater
replies.text:
+Ezequiel Teruel  could be an external microphone wasn't plugged fully in, a stereo jack sockety with the plug not fully inserted can make contact with only one channel. Similar effect to a broken wire in one channel.
Interesting room acoustics also make this 'interesting' - it's not unreadable but does sound a little strange.
